attributes.stem:
An 18-year-old woman is brought into the Emergency Department. Over the past three days, she has noted progressive ascending weakness in the lower limbs and is now unable to mobilise. She was unwell with a prolonged course of diarrhoea three weeks ago. This was managed conservatively. She had returned from an overseas trip several days prior to this. She denies any current diarrhoea, coryzal symptoms, or pain. On examination, her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 110/60mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8C. Her cranial nerve examination is unremarkable. There is normal tone, decreased power, and absent reflexes in her lower limbs, with downgoing plantars bilaterally. Perianal sensation and tone are intact. There is mild stocking distribution sensory loss in both her legs up to her mid-calf. Vibration has been lost up to her knee bilaterally. Her gait is unsteady but not ataxic.
Her blood tests are unremarkable excepting a C-reactive protein of 20 mg/L (normal <3.0mg/L).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the next most appropriate investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS), which is an acute demyelinating polyneuropathy with suspected autoimmune aetiology. It often presents as an acute, paralysing illness provoked by a preceding infection. There are many variant forms of GBS. The immune response is directed towards the myelin or axon of the peripheral nerve. Infection with campylobacter jejuni is a commonly identified precipitant, although cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, human immunodeficiency virus, and Zika virus have also been associated. The supportive signs include progressive, symmetric muscle weakness (extremities, facial, respiratory, and/or bulbar muscles) with onset over hours to days, with absent or depressed deep tendon reflexes. The weakness usually begins in the legs. In 10-30 percent of cases, severe respiratory muscle weakness develops, and autonomic instability can occur. Sensory symptoms are often present although objective sensory signs are usually mild relative to the motor weakness. The C-reactive protein is non-specific and may reflect underlying inflammation.

The initial diagnosis of GBS is based on clinical presentation. CSF analysis and nerve conduction studies may be used to support the diagnosis. Lumbar puncture should be performed in the first instance for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis. The typical finding in GBS is an elevated CSF protein with normal white blood cell count - a finding called albuminocytological dissociation, which is seen in 66 percent of patients with GBS. This, however, is not diagnostic but may aid in the clinical picture. Nerve conduction studies, which require more specialised technical skill and equipment, may be performed at a later stage, and may help to differentiate between the main variants of GBS - either by showing predominantly demyelinating features in acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP - which is the most common form) or axonal features in acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN) or acute sensorimotor axonal neuropathy (AMSAN). Furthermore, findings on such studies may be normal in the early stages of GBS.

Neuroimaging (CT or MRI) of the spine may reveal abnormalities such as thickening and enhancement of the intrathecal spinal nerve roots and cauda equina. However, these are not diagnostic. Ganglioside antibody testing (specifically for anti-GQ1b) is useful for diagnosis of Miller Fisher syndrome, Bickerstaff encephalitis, or the pharyngeal-cervical brachial GBS variant. Nerve conduction studies may be helpful in classifying the variant of GBS, but are not necessary for diagnosis.

Stool culture may reveal a causative agent for the recent infection but is not pathognomonic for GBS

GBS occurs in 1 to 2 people per population of 100,000 and is treated acutely with plasmapheresis or intravenous immunoglobulin to suppress the inflammatory response. Recovery varies depending on the severity of illness, typically taking weeks to months, and some patients are left with persistent deficits. Ongoing clinical deterioration or relapse after eight weeks would be consistent with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP).
learningPoints: The key clinical features of Guillain Barre syndrome is acute progressive (typically ascending) weakness accompanied by areflexia.
The most appropriate initial investigation in Guillain Barre syndrome is lumbar puncture to assess for albuminocytological dissociation.
references: Chandrashekhar, S., Dimachkie, M. M., & Rabinstein, A. A. Guillain-Barré syndrome in adults: Pathogenesis, clinical features, and diagnosis.

Rath, J., Zulehner, G., Schober, B., Grisold, A., Krenn, M., Cetin, H., & Zimprich, F. (2021). Cerebrospinal fluid analysis in Guillain–Barré syndrome: value of albumin quotients. Journal of Neurology, 268(9), 3294-3300.
